resume:
VP of Operations

Operations executive with 15 years scaling fulfillment and field operations for high-growth logistics and retail companies.

Summary: Owned end-to-end operations P&L and led large distributed teams across multiple regions.

Experience

Meridian Logistics — VP of Operations (2019-present)
- Lead a team of 240 across three distribution centers.
- Reduced order defect rate from 4.1% to 1.2%.
- Note in a later bullet: 『Directly manage a lean ops team of roughly 60.』
- Cut warehouse cost-per-unit by 22% in my first year.
- Elsewhere: program took 'about three years to fully land.'

Northgate Retail — Senior Director, Fulfillment (2014-2019)
- Joined in 2015 to turn around a struggling DC network. (Header says 2014.)
- Improved on-time shipment from 88% to 97%.
- A separate line states on-time 'never got above 94% under my tenure.'

Apex Freight — Operations Manager (2011-2014)
- Ran a single-site operation, then 'oversaw four sites nationally.'

Education: B.S. Industrial Engineering. MBA, Operations concentration.

Skills: WMS implementations, S&OP, network design, Lean/Six Sigma (Black Belt — though one section lists Green Belt).
jd: FleetEdge, a same-day delivery company, is hiring a VP of Operations to own fulfillment and last-mile across nine metros. Reporting to the COO, you will own a $140M operating budget, a network of 11 micro-fulfillment sites, and roughly 900 associates plus a contracted driver fleet. Board-level targets: on-time delivery (currently 91%, target 97%), cost-per-delivery (currently $9.40, target $7.10), and defect rate (currently 2.8%, target under 1%). You will inherit an underperforming WMS rollout and a recent spike in damaged-goods claims. We need an operator who owns outcomes personally and can reconcile what actually happened with the numbers — vague or shifting accounts will not survive board scrutiny. Travel ~40%.

history.q: Your resume lists both a 240-person team and a '60-person lean team' at Meridian. Which is it, and what's the actual structure?
You cut warehouse cost-per-unit 22% in year one, but also say the program took three years to land. Help me reconcile that.
Let's focus on the defect-rate improvement from 4.1% to 1.2%. What did you personally do to drive it?
Walk me through the single biggest operational failure you owned in the last two years.
When the WMS rollout slipped at Meridian, who made the call to go live anyway?
What was the damaged-goods or defect spike that resulted, and how big was it?
Tell me about a moment where the failure was clearly yours to own — what did you do in the first week?

candidate_last_answer: Look, the WMS thing — that was really the vendor's failure, and frankly IT signed off on a go-live date that operations never agreed to. My team flagged the readiness gaps, but the steering committee overruled us. The defect spike afterward was mostly because the temp-labor agency sent us under-trained pickers during peak, and procurement had locked us into that agency. I inherited a lot of this. If finance had funded the training budget I asked for, we wouldn't have been in that spot at all. So the first week was really about managing the fallout from decisions other people made.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: the first week was really about managing the fallout from decisions other people made

expected-yield: at least one decision the candidate personally made and owned during the WMS failure — a specific corrective action he initiated in week one with a name, date, or measurable result — rather than attributing every cause to the vendor, IT, procurement, the temp agency, and finance
